1: YHWH mî-yāgûr bĕʾohŏlekā (YHWH mî-yāḡûr bĕʾohŏleḵā): 'O LORD, who shall sojourn in your tent?' Psalm 15 is a torah-liturgy or entrance psalm — an answer to the question posed at the sanctuary gate, identifying who may enter to worship. The noun ʾōhel ('tent') recalls the wilderness tabernacle and its successor at Jerusalem; har qodšekā ('your holy hill') is Zion/Mount Moriah. The double question — who may sojourn (gûr, temporary residence), who may dwell (šākan, settled habitation) — parallels Psalms 24:3 ('who shall ascend the hill of the LORD?'). The answer is not ethnic or ritual but ethical.
2: hôlēk tāmîm ûpōʿēl ṣedeq (hôlēḵ tāmîm ûp̄ōʿēl ṣeḏeq): 'He who walks blamelessly and does what is right.' The present participles — hôlēk ('one who walks'), pōʿēl ('one who does'), dōbēr ('one who speaks') — describe character as persistent disposition rather than isolated acts. The noun tāmîm ('blameless, complete, without defect') is used of sacrificial animals without blemish (Leviticus 1:3) and of Noah (Genesis 6:9) and Abraham (Genesis 17:1) — moral completeness. The phrase wĕdōbēr ʾĕmet bĕlibābô, 'who speaks truth in his heart,' combines speech and interiority: the truthful person does not merely say true things but thinks truly.
3: lōʾ-rāgal ʿal-lĕšōnô (lōʾ-rāḡal ʿal-lĕšōnô): 'who does not slander with his tongue.' The verb rāgal ('to spy, go as a foot-soldier') in the piel means 'to slander, go about as a tale-bearer' — the image of someone who covertly undermines others. Three negatives characterize the qualified worshipper: no slander, no evil done to a neighbor, no reproach taken up against a friend. The phrase lōʾ nāśāʾ ḥerpâ ʿal-qĕrôbô ('does not take up a reproach against his neighbor') suggests not repeating or amplifying rumors about those close to him.
4: nibzeh bĕʿênāyw nimʾās (niḇzeh bĕʿênāyw nimʾās): 'in whose eyes a vile person is despised.' The niphal nibzeh ('despised, held in contempt') and nimʾās ('rejected, disdained') — the righteous person's moral perception correctly evaluates character rather than status. The contrast: wĕʾet-yirʾê YHWH yĕkabbēd, 'but he honors those who fear the LORD.' Moral evaluation follows theological reality, not social prestige. The crucial phrase: nišbaʿ lĕhāraʿ wĕlōʾ yāmîr, 'who swears to his own hurt and does not change.' The righteous person keeps an oath even when it costs him — the ultimate test of verbal integrity (cf. Matthew 5:37).
5: kaspô lōʾ nātan bĕnešek (ḵaspô lōʾ nāṯan bĕnešeḵ): 'who does not put out his money at interest.' The noun nešek ('interest, biting') from nāšak ('to bite') describes usury — taking a bite from the poor through compound interest. The combination of nešek and tarbît ('increase') in Leviticus 25:36-37 was explicitly prohibited in lending to the poor within Israel. The final clause: wĕšōḥad ʿal-nāqî lōʾ lāqaḥ, 'who does not take a bribe against the innocent.' The word šōḥad ('bribe') is the corruption of the justice system that should protect the nāqî ('innocent'). The closing promise — ʿōśēh ʾēlleh lōʾ yimmôṭ lĕʿôlām, 'the one who does these things shall never be moved' — echoes lōʾ yimmôṭ of Psalms 13:4 (the enemy's boast), now granted to the covenant-faithful as divine assurance.
